title: Distance Metrics & Similarity Thresholds
concept: Recognition models compute L2 (Euclidean) distance or Cosine Similarity between face embeddings to determine if they match.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main advantage of face embeddings over raw face pixels?

options.text: They are compact vectors robust to lighting and pose changes.
They are larger in size, capturing more pixels.
They are easy to display on the screen directly.

exercises.explain: Correct! Embeddings are low-dimensional representations that remain stable even if lighting or position changes.

cards.title: How close is a match?
cards.body: When your phone scans your face to unlock, it gets a new vector that doesn't match the stored one *exactly*. Shadows, a new haircut, or a smile shift the numbers slightly. The phone must calculate a distance between these vectors and ask: is this close enough to be the same person?

cards.title: Distance and Similarity
cards.body: We compare embeddings using two main metrics: Euclidean Distance (L2) (measuring the straight-line distance between two points) and Cosine Similarity (measuring the angle between two vectors). Cosine similarity ranges from -1 (opposite) to 1 (identical).

cards.title: The ruler vs. the compass
cards.body: Euclidean distance is like a ruler: it measures the physical gap between two points in space. Cosine similarity is like a compass: it doesn't care how far apart they are, only if they are pointing in the exact same direction.

cards.title: Setting a threshold
cards.body: In a verification app, you define a **similarity threshold** (e.g. Cosine Similarity > 0.75). If a scanned face has a similarity score of 0.82, the lock opens. If the score is 0.60, access is rejected.

cards.title: Distance is never zero
cards.body: A common myth is that two different photos of you will have a distance of zero. Because of tiny changes in expression, lighting, and camera noise, the vectors will never be identical. We must always use a threshold rather than looking for a perfect match.

cards.title: L2 Distance vs. Cosine Similarity
cards.body: How the two metrics measure vector alignment:

left.text:
Measures straight-line gap
Same person: Low distance
Different people: High distance

right.text:
Measures vector angle
Same person: High score (~0.85)
Different people: Low score (~0.20)

exercises.prompt: If a face recognition model uses Cosine Similarity, what score indicates the closest match?
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, ranging from -1 to 1. 1.0 means identical alignment.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a user wears glasses, their face embedding's distance to the stored template will remain exactly 0.0.
exercises.explain: False. Minor changes like glasses or lighting alter the embedding values slightly, yielding a small non-zero distance.

exercises.sentence: Euclidean distance measures the physical gap, whereas cosine similarity measures the vector ___.
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity evaluates the angular direction between two vectors rather than their magnitude.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a threshold comparison that grants access in a verification system?

options.text: Stored vs. scanned Cosine Similarity = 0.85 (Threshold = 0.80) -> Access Granted
Stored vs. scanned Euclidean Distance = 4.2 (Threshold = 1.5) -> Access Granted

exercises.explain: When Cosine Similarity exceeds the threshold, it indicates a match, granting access.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a banking app's face lock. During testing, family members are occasionally able to unlock each other's accounts. What threshold adjustment should you make?

options.text: Increase the Cosine Similarity threshold (or lower the L2 distance threshold) to make the matching criteria stricter.
Decrease the Cosine Similarity threshold to 0.2 to allow anyone to match.
Disable alignment pre-processing to make inputs random.
Increase the learning rate of the camera's auto-focus.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Stricter thresholds prevent unauthorized matches (False Accepts) at the cost of occasionally rejecting the actual user (False Rejects). Setting this trade-off is critical. Excellent! Next: how we train models to output these close embeddings.
